lists.title: 10 Best Parks in ITP Atlanta
lists.seoTitle: 10 Best Parks in ITP Atlanta | atlanta news & talk
lists.seoDescription: The best parks and green spaces inside the perimeter in Atlanta, from Piedmont Park to lesser-known gems. A local's guide to getting outside ITP.
lists.intro:
Atlanta is one of the most heavily forested cities in the country, and the parks here reflect that. You can walk through old-growth canopy minutes from downtown, find hidden creeks in neighborhoods most visitors never see, and actually enjoy being outside for a solid nine months of the year.

This list covers the best parks ITP (Inside The Perimeter, for the uninitiated). These are places locals actually go, not just the ones that show up on tourist brochures. Whether you want a trail run, a picnic spot, or somewhere to let the kids burn off energy, there's a park on this list for you.
lists.imageAlt: Lush green trees and walking trail in an Atlanta park
lists.imageCredit: Photo by Luca Bravo on Unsplash

items.name: Piedmont Park
items.description: The crown jewel of Atlanta parks. 200 acres of green space right in Midtown with skyline views that never get old. The Active Oval, Lake Clara Meer, and the Saturday Green Market are all worth your time. It connects directly to the BeltLine Eastside Trail, so you can walk or bike over from multiple neighborhoods.
items.tip: The northern end near The Meadow is way less crowded than the southern entrance. Park on Monroe Drive and enter from there.

items.name: Grant Park
items.description: One of Atlanta's oldest parks, right next to Zoo Atlanta. The park itself is 131 acres with great walking paths, a historic gateway, and excellent people watching. The Sunday farmers market at the park is a neighborhood staple.
items.tip: The loop trail around the park is about 1.5 miles and mostly shaded. Great for an easy morning jog.

items.name: Freedom Park
items.description: A linear park that connects the Carter Center to the BeltLine, cutting through Poncey-Highland, Candler Park, and Little Five Points. It's a greenway with paved paths perfect for biking, running, or just a walk. The section near Moreland Avenue has some beautiful mature trees.
items.tip: Use the Freedom Park path to bike from L5P all the way to Piedmont Park without touching a single car road.

items.name: Historic Fourth Ward Park
items.description: A relatively new park (opened 2011) that turned a flood-prone area into one of the best spots in town. The splash pad, the amphitheater, and the stormwater pond that doubles as a scenic lake are all excellent. Sits right on the Eastside Trail.
items.tip: Grab a coffee from Dancing Goats next door at Ponce City Market and walk the loop trail. Hard to beat on a Saturday morning.

items.name: Westside Park
items.description: Atlanta's newest major park and the largest greenspace in the city at 280 acres. Built on the site of the old Bellwood Quarry, the views of the reservoir from the overlook are genuinely stunning. It still feels like a secret to many Atlantans.
items.tip: Head straight to the reservoir overlook when you arrive. The quarry water is an unreal shade of blue-green.

items.name: Candler Park
items.description: A beloved neighborhood park with a pool, tennis courts, a golf course, and a playground. The disc golf course here is one of the most popular in the city. The park has a great community feel, especially during the annual Candler Park Fall Fest.
items.tip: The disc golf course is free to play. Bring your own discs or borrow from a friendly local on the first tee.

items.name: Olmsted Linear Park
items.description: A chain of connected parks designed by the Olmsted Brothers firm (the same family behind Central Park). Runs through Druid Hills along Ponce de Leon Avenue with gorgeous old trees, open meadows, and very little foot traffic compared to bigger parks.
items.tip: Walk the full length from Virgilee Park to Deepdene Park for a peaceful two-mile stroll through some of Atlanta's prettiest scenery.

items.name: Fernbank Forest
items.description: 65 acres of old-growth forest right in the middle of the city. Seriously. Some of these trees are over 200 years old. Access is through Fernbank Museum (with admission) or the free WildWoods paths. It feels like you've been transported to the mountains.
items.tip: The WildWoods outdoor experience is free and has a paved loop trail that's great for kids. The museum trail requires admission but is worth it.

items.name: Brownwood Park
items.description: A small, well-loved park in East Atlanta with a playground, tennis courts, and a community garden. It's the kind of neighborhood park where everyone knows each other's dogs. The mature tree canopy provides solid shade even in August.
items.tip: The community garden plots sometimes have openings. Check with the East Atlanta Community Association if you're interested.

items.name: Coan Park
items.description: Tucked into the East Lake neighborhood, Coan Park has a recently renovated playground, walking paths, and a rec center. It's never crowded and the rolling terrain makes for a decent workout. A true hidden gem.
items.tip: The walking loop has some real hills. If you want a tougher walk than the flat BeltLine, this is your spot.

lists.title: Best Coffee Shops Along the BeltLine
lists.seoTitle: Best Coffee Shops Along the Atlanta BeltLine | atlanta news & talk
lists.seoDescription: The best coffee shops on or near the Atlanta BeltLine trail. Real spots locals hit for espresso, cold brew, and work-from-cafe vibes.
lists.intro:
The BeltLine is a 22-mile loop of trails, parks, and transit that's reshaping Atlanta. It's also lined with some of the best coffee in the city. Whether you're mid-walk and need a caffeine stop or looking for a solid work-from-cafe spot with BeltLine access, these are the places to know.

This list focuses on shops that are either directly on the trail or a very short walk from it. All of them are worth a dedicated trip, not just a convenience stop.
lists.imageAlt: Interior of a cozy coffee shop with natural light
lists.imageCredit: Photo by Mike Kenneally on Unsplash

items.name: Dancing Goats Coffee Bar (Ponce City Market)
items.description: Batdorf & Bronson's flagship Atlanta location, right at the central hub of the Eastside Trail. The space is beautiful, the espresso is consistently excellent, and the patio overlooks the BeltLine. You can watch the whole parade of joggers, dogs, and strollers while you sip.
items.tip: The cold brew here is outstanding. Skip the line inside and order at the outdoor window when it's open.

items.name: Revelator Coffee (Westside Trail)
items.description: A Birmingham-born roaster with a sleek outpost on the Westside Trail near Lee + White. The pour-overs are precise and the space has a cool industrial feel. Great place to post up with a laptop.
items.tip: Try the seasonal single-origin pour-over. The baristas here actually know their stuff and can guide you.

items.name: Chrome Yellow Trading Co.
items.description: A coffee shop and general store on Krog Street, steps from the Eastside Trail. The vibe is part neighborhood hangout, part design store. Their drip coffee is simple and good, and they stock interesting local goods you won't find elsewhere.
items.tip: Grab a coffee and walk over to the Krog Street Tunnel to see the latest street art. It changes constantly.

items.name: Muchacho
items.description: A tiny takeaway window in Summerhill that punches way above its weight. The cortado is one of the best in Atlanta, and the breakfast tacos are legit. It's a quick detour from the Southside Trail section of the BeltLine.
items.tip: Cash and card accepted. The line moves fast. Get the breakfast tacos with your coffee, you won't regret it.

items.name: Brash Coffee
items.description: Located in Ponce City Market, Brash is laser-focused on espresso. The menu is short by design: espresso, macchiato, cortado, latte. That's it. And every single one is dialed in. If you care about the craft, this is your spot.
items.tip: Ask for the guest espresso if they have one running. Brash frequently features rotating roasters.

items.name: Hodgepodge Coffeehouse
items.description: A cozy, slightly funky neighborhood spot in East Atlanta Village with a solid patio. The vibe is relaxed, the coffee is good, and it's a short ride from the planned Southeast Trail. Great for a low-key afternoon.
items.tip: Try the Thai iced tea if you want something different. The patio is dog-friendly and shaded.

items.name: Docent Coffee
items.description: Docent has two locations near BeltLine-adjacent neighborhoods: one on the Westside and one in the Ford Factory Lofts in Poncey-Highland. Both serve excellent specialty coffee. The Ford Factory spot is a quick walk to the Eastside Trail.
items.tip: The Ford Factory location has limited seating but a great window counter for people watching on North Avenue.

items.name: Little Tart Bakeshop
items.description: Located in the Krog Street Market, just off the BeltLine. Not purely a coffee shop, but the espresso is serious and the pastries are some of the best in Atlanta. The almond croissant is famous for a reason.
items.tip: Get there before 10am on weekends or the almond croissants will be gone. Not an exaggeration.

items.name: East Pole Coffee Co.
items.description: A roaster and cafe in the Armour Yards area near the Eastside Trail's northern section. The space is open and airy, the beans are roasted on site, and there's plenty of room to spread out with a laptop. One of the best work-from-cafe setups in Atlanta.
items.tip: Buy a bag of their house blend to take home. It's a great everyday coffee and roasted fresh.

items.name: Taproom Coffee
items.description: A Kirkwood favorite that's close to the planned eastward BeltLine extension. Taproom is all about community: they host events, support local artists, and the coffee is consistently solid. The outdoor space is generous.
items.tip: Check their Instagram for pop-up events. They regularly host local vendors and live music.

lists.title: Top Restaurants in Old Fourth Ward
lists.seoTitle: Top Restaurants in Old Fourth Ward Atlanta | atlanta news & talk
lists.seoDescription: The best restaurants in Old Fourth Ward (O4W), Atlanta. From Ponce City Market favorites to neighborhood gems, these are the spots locals love.
lists.intro:
Old Fourth Ward has gone through one of the most dramatic transformations of any Atlanta neighborhood in the last decade. The BeltLine Eastside Trail runs right through it, Ponce City Market anchors the north end, and new restaurants seem to open every month. But the best spots here aren't all new. Some have been around for years, surviving and thriving through all the changes.

This list covers the restaurants worth your time in O4W, from the heavy hitters to the under-the-radar spots that deserve more attention.
lists.imageAlt: Warm restaurant interior with ambient lighting
lists.imageCredit: Photo by Jason Leung on Unsplash

items.name: Staplehouse
items.description: One of Atlanta's most celebrated restaurants. The tasting menu changes regularly and features creative dishes built from seasonal Southern ingredients. The space is intimate and the service is warm without being fussy. Staplehouse also supports the Giving Kitchen nonprofit, so your meal goes toward a good cause.
items.tip: Reservations are essential and can be tough to get. Check their site on Monday mornings when new dates drop.

items.name: Tabla
items.description: Indian cuisine with a modern approach, located right on the Eastside Trail. The dosas are excellent, the cocktail menu is thoughtful, and the BeltLine-facing patio is hard to beat for people watching. This is a great date night spot.
items.tip: Sit on the BeltLine-side patio during sunset. The lamb rogan josh is one of the best entrees on the menu.

items.name: Barcelona Wine Bar
items.description: Spanish tapas and an extensive wine list in a lively, warm space. The patatas bravas are a must, the cheese boards are well-curated, and the weekend brunch is excellent. It gets loud in the best way.
items.tip: The happy hour specials on wine and select tapas are a genuine deal. Go early on a weeknight.

items.name: Ponce City Market Food Hall
items.description: Not a single restaurant but a collection of excellent stalls under one roof. Highlights include H&F Burger (from the Holeman & Finch team), Minero (Sean Brock's taco spot), Ton Ton (ramen and dumplings), and Honeysuckle Gelato. It's a food hall done right.
items.tip: Skip the crowds on the main level and head upstairs to the roof. The views of Midtown from The Roof at PCM are worth the elevator ride.

items.name: Vortex Bar & Grill
items.description: A Midtown/L5P institution, but the original location at 438 Moreland is the one with the giant skull entrance. The burgers are massive, the atmosphere is unapologetically dive-bar-meets-burger-joint, and they have a strict no-kids policy. It's an experience.
items.tip: Get the Coronary Bypass burger if you're feeling brave. It's multiple patties with fried eggs and all the fixings.

items.name: BoccaLupo
items.description: Handmade pasta in a cozy, unpretentious space on North Highland. The corzetti with pork sugo is a standout, and the menu changes often based on what's available. It's the kind of place that makes you wonder why you ever eat pasta from a box.
items.tip: Sit at the bar and watch the pasta being made. It's mesmerizing and the bartenders are great conversationalists.

items.name: Wrecking Bar Brewpub
items.description: A brewpub set in the basement of a restored Victorian mansion. The beer is brewed on-site and the food goes way beyond typical pub fare. The smoked wings and the burger are both excellent. The space itself is worth the visit.
items.tip: Try whatever seasonal beer is on tap. The brewers here are creative and the limited releases sell out fast.

items.name: Ammazza
items.description: Neapolitan-style pizza cooked in a massive wood-fired oven. The dough is made with imported Italian flour and fermented for 72 hours. The margherita is perfect in its simplicity, and the burrata appetizer is rich enough to split.
items.tip: They don't take reservations and it gets packed. Arrive before 6pm on weeknights to avoid the wait.

items.name: Poor Hendrix
items.description: A cocktail-forward restaurant with a seasonal menu that changes frequently. The small plates are inventive without being gimmicky, and the drink program is one of the best in the neighborhood. The space is intimate and dimly lit in that good-date way.
items.tip: The bartender's choice cocktail is always a good call. Tell them what you like and they'll make something custom.

items.name: Krog Street Market
items.description: Another food hall, this one in a converted warehouse with a BeltLine entrance. Standouts include Gu's Dumplings (legendary Chinese dumplings), Fred's Meat & Bread (incredible sandwiches), and Jeni's Splendid Ice Creams. More focused than PCM and less touristy.
items.tip: Gu's Dumplings always has a line, and it's always worth the wait. Get the spicy wontons and pan-fried pork dumplings.

lists.title: Best Brunch Spots in Decatur
lists.seoTitle: Best Brunch Spots in Decatur GA | atlanta news & talk
lists.seoDescription: The best brunch restaurants in Decatur, Georgia. From classic Southern breakfast to creative weekend menus, these are the spots Decatur locals love.
lists.intro:
Decatur punches way above its weight when it comes to food, and brunch is no exception. This walkable little city just east of Atlanta has a restaurant scene that rivals neighborhoods three times its size. The square and the surrounding blocks are packed with places that take weekend brunch seriously.

Whether you want biscuits and gravy, a creative eggs Benedict, or a boozy Bloody Mary on a patio, Decatur has you covered. These are the spots that locals actually go to, not just the ones that photograph well.
lists.imageAlt: Bright brunch restaurant with plates of food on table
lists.imageCredit: Photo by Jason Leung on Unsplash

items.name: Revival
items.description: Kevin Gillespie's Southern restaurant on the Decatur square does a brunch that's hard to top. The fried chicken biscuit is legendary, the shrimp and grits are textbook, and the pastry case will test your willpower. The patio overlooking the square is prime real estate.
items.tip: Go early. By 10:30am on Saturdays there's usually a wait. The pastry basket is worth adding to any order.

items.name: Brick Store Pub
items.description: Known primarily as a beer bar, but the Brick Store's brunch is an underrated gem. The menu is small and focused, the bloody mary is excellent, and the Belgian-style space upstairs feels like a proper European pub breakfast. A Decatur institution since 1997.
items.tip: Head upstairs to the Belgian bar for a quieter experience. Pair your brunch with one of their curated Belgian ales.

items.name: Sun in My Belly
items.description: A Kirkwood gem that's technically just outside Decatur proper but close enough to count. The menu uses lots of local produce, the baked goods are made in-house, and the garden patio is one of the most pleasant brunch settings in metro Atlanta.
items.tip: The French toast with seasonal fruit compote is a regular favorite. Call ahead for weekend reservations.

items.name: White Bull
items.description: Italian-leaning brunch in a charming space on Church Street. The frittata is always well-executed, and the brunch cocktail menu is creative without being over the top. It's a refined brunch spot that doesn't take itself too seriously.
items.tip: The house-made focaccia is excellent. Ask if they have any fresh pasta specials running at brunch.

items.name: The Iberian Pig
items.description: Spanish tapas-style brunch with a killer cocktail menu. The churros with chocolate are a must, and the savory egg dishes have a Spanish twist that sets them apart from every other brunch spot in town. The space is cozy and energetic.
items.tip: The bottomless sangria brunch deal is one of the best values in Decatur. Make a reservation.

items.name: Cafe Alsace
items.description: An Alsatian-inspired cafe on West Ponce de Leon with excellent pastries, savory tarts, and proper French coffee. The quiche is consistently perfect, and the outdoor seating area is relaxed and dog-friendly.
items.tip: The tarte flambee (Alsatian flatbread) is different from anything else you'll find at brunch in Decatur.

items.name: Community Q BBQ
items.description: A barbecue joint doing brunch might sound unusual, but Community Q makes it work. Brisket hash, smoked meat omelets, and some of the best biscuits in Decatur. It's not fancy, but the quality speaks for itself.
items.tip: The brisket hash with eggs is the move. Don't overthink it.

items.name: Saba Italian Bar + Kitchen
items.description: A newer Italian spot on the Decatur square with a weekend brunch that's gaining a following. The lemon ricotta pancakes are a standout, and the espresso program is legit. It's a solid addition to the Decatur brunch scene.
items.tip: Sit at the bar for a more relaxed vibe and faster service. The baristas pull proper espresso.

lists.title: Best Bars in East Atlanta Village
lists.seoTitle: Best Bars in East Atlanta Village (EAV) | atlanta news & talk
lists.seoDescription: The best bars in East Atlanta Village, from dive bars to craft cocktail spots. A local's guide to drinking in EAV.
lists.intro:
East Atlanta Village is one of the last truly gritty, character-filled bar districts in ITP Atlanta. While other neighborhoods have gone full-polished, EAV holds onto its rough edges, and that's exactly what makes it great. The bars here range from legendary dives to solid craft cocktail spots, and the whole strip is walkable.

If you want a night out that doesn't feel like a corporate restaurant group planned it, EAV is your move. Here are the bars worth hitting.
lists.imageAlt: Dimly lit bar interior with bottles on shelves
lists.imageCredit: Photo by Stanislav Ivanitskiy on Unsplash

items.name: The EARL
items.description: The anchor of EAV's bar and music scene. The EARL is a dive bar and music venue that's been hosting indie bands, punk shows, and everything in between for over two decades. The burgers are surprisingly good, the patio is a classic hang, and the music calendar is always worth checking.
items.tip: Check the live music schedule on their website. Some of the best shows in Atlanta happen here on random Tuesday nights.

items.name: Argosy
items.description: A gastropub with a deep beer list, solid cocktails, and elevated bar food. The space is large with a great patio, and it's the kind of place where you can bring a date or meet up with a group. The burger and the Nashville hot chicken sandwich are both excellent.
items.tip: The back patio has fire pits that are perfect on cooler nights. Arrive early on weekends to snag a spot.

items.name: Gaja Korean Bar
items.description: Korean-inspired cocktails and bar snacks in a stylish space that adds some variety to the EAV strip. The soju cocktails are fun, the fried chicken is outstanding, and the late-night menu is a lifesaver after a night of bar hopping.
items.tip: The Korean fried chicken wings are a must. Order them with the gochujang sauce.

items.name: Flatiron
items.description: A cocktail bar and restaurant in the historic Flatiron building at the center of the village. The drink menu is creative and seasonal, the food is solid New American, and the rooftop patio has great views of the EAV intersection.
items.tip: Head to the rooftop for sunset. It's one of the better rooftop views in east Atlanta.

items.name: Mary's
items.description: An LGBTQ+ bar and dance spot that's been a cornerstone of EAV for years. The energy is always high, the DJs know what they're doing, and it's one of the most welcoming bars in the city. The drag shows are legendary.
items.tip: The drag shows on weekends are not to be missed. Get there early for a good spot.

items.name: The Midway Pub
items.description: A proper dive bar with cheap beer, pool tables, and zero pretense. The Midway is where you go when you want a $4 PBR and a game of pool without any fuss. The jukebox is solid and the regulars are friendly.
items.tip: Cash is king here. There's an ATM inside but bring your own to avoid the fee.

items.name: Banshee
items.description: Craft cocktails and a curated wine list in a moody, intimate space. Banshee is the more refined option on the EAV strip. The bartenders are knowledgeable, the drink menu changes with the seasons, and the small plates pair well with drinks.
items.tip: Tell the bartender what spirits you like and let them make you something off-menu. They're good at it.

items.name: Crown and Conch
items.description: A newer bar and restaurant in EAV with a tropical-meets-Southern vibe. The rum cocktails are strong and creative, the seafood is surprisingly good for a bar, and the patio is a nice hangout in warmer months.
items.tip: Try the rum punch. It's dangerously easy to drink on a hot day.

lists.title: Top Things to Do on the Eastside Trail
lists.seoTitle: Top Things to Do on the Atlanta BeltLine Eastside Trail | atlanta news & talk
lists.seoDescription: The best activities, stops, and things to do along the Atlanta BeltLine Eastside Trail from Piedmont Park to Inman Park.
lists.intro:
The Eastside Trail is the most popular section of the Atlanta BeltLine, running about 2.25 miles from Piedmont Park down to Inman Park. It's the stretch that put the BeltLine on the map, and it's packed with things to see, eat, and do along the way.

You can walk the whole thing in about 45 minutes, but you'll want to budget way more time than that. There are too many good stops to just blow through it. Here are the best things to do along the Eastside Trail, roughly in order from north to south.
lists.imageAlt: Urban trail path surrounded by greenery
lists.imageCredit: Photo by Tomas Martinez on Unsplash

items.name: Piedmont Park Access Point
items.description: Start your walk at the southern end of Piedmont Park where the trail connects. The transition from park to trail is seamless, and you get some of the best skyline views right at the beginning. There are usually food vendors and buskers near the entry point.
items.tip: Start early on weekend mornings (before 9am) to beat the crowds. The trail gets packed by mid-morning on nice days.

items.name: Ponce City Market
items.description: The massive mixed-use development in the old Sears building is the biggest attraction on the trail. The food hall has dozens of vendors, the shops are worth browsing, and The Roof amusement area on top has carnival games and skyline views.
items.tip: The Central Food Hall on the main floor has the best food options. Skip the sit-down restaurants and graze the stalls instead.

items.name: Historic Fourth Ward Park
items.description: This award-winning park sits right on the trail between Ponce City Market and the Inman Park section. The splash pad is perfect for kids in summer, and the walking loop around the stormwater pond is a nice detour from the main trail.
items.tip: Bring a blanket and sit on the hill overlooking the pond. It's one of the most relaxing spots on the whole BeltLine.

items.name: BeltLine Art Installations
items.description: Art on the Atlanta BeltLine is a curated public art program that commissions temporary and permanent installations along the trail. The murals, sculptures, and interactive pieces change regularly. You'll see new stuff every few months.
items.tip: Download the BeltLine app to get info on current installations. Some of the interactive pieces are easy to walk right past.

items.name: Krog Street Tunnel
items.description: The famous graffiti tunnel that passes under railroad tracks near the southern end of the Eastside Trail. It's a constantly evolving gallery of street art, tags, and murals. It's loud, colorful, and distinctly Atlanta.
items.tip: The art changes weekly. If you see something you love, photograph it because it might be painted over by next week.

items.name: Krog Street Market
items.description: A food hall in a converted warehouse at the southern end of the Eastside Trail. Smaller and more intimate than Ponce City Market. Highlights include Gu's Dumplings, Fred's Meat & Bread, and Jeni's Ice Creams.
items.tip: The back patio connects to the trail and is a great place to eat with a view of the BeltLine traffic.

items.name: New Realm Brewing
items.description: A massive brewery with a rooftop patio directly on the BeltLine. The beers are solid, the food menu is more extensive than most breweries, and the views of the Eastside Trail from above are great for watching the sunset.
items.tip: The rooftop fills up fast on weekends. Head up there first to grab a table, then order at the bar.

items.name: Bike Rentals and Scooter Stations
items.description: Multiple bike and scooter rental stations sit along the trail. Relay Bike Share has docking stations at several points, and Bird/Lime scooters are scattered throughout. Riding the trail is a completely different experience than walking it.
items.tip: Rent a bike over a scooter. The trail has some narrow sections where scooters at full speed can be a hazard for pedestrians.

items.name: Inman Park Neighborhood
items.description: The southern terminus of the Eastside Trail puts you right in Inman Park, one of Atlanta's most beautiful neighborhoods. Walk the tree-lined streets to see some incredible Victorian architecture. The Inman Park Festival in April is one of the city's best events.
items.tip: Walk down Elizabeth Street for some of the best Victorian homes in Atlanta. The butterfly sculpture at the corner is a popular photo spot.

items.name: Paris on Ponce
items.description: A massive vintage and antique store on North Highland Avenue, a short walk from the trail. It's 50,000 square feet of curated chaos: old signs, furniture, art, oddities, and one-of-a-kind finds. Even if you don't buy anything, it's worth the browse.
items.tip: Allow more time than you think. You can easily spend an hour wandering through the different rooms and vendor booths.

lists.title: Best Pizza in Atlanta ITP
lists.seoTitle: Best Pizza in Atlanta ITP | atlanta news & talk
lists.seoDescription: The best pizza spots inside the perimeter in Atlanta, from Neapolitan wood-fired to New York slices and Detroit-style. A local's pizza guide.
lists.intro:
Atlanta's pizza scene has come a long way. You can now get legitimate Neapolitan, proper New York-style slices, Detroit-style pan pizza, and creative pies that don't fit into any category. All inside the perimeter.

This list covers the pizza places that are actually worth driving across town for. Not chains, not franchises. These are independent spots run by people who care about dough, sauce, and cheese in that order.
lists.imageAlt: Close-up of a freshly baked pizza with melted cheese
lists.imageCredit: Photo by Ivan Torres on Unsplash

items.name: Antico Pizza Napoletana
items.description: The place that put Atlanta on the pizza map. Giovanni Di Palma's wood-fired Neapolitan pies are the real deal, made with imported Italian ingredients and cooked at 900 degrees. The San Gennaro (sausage, peppers, onions) is outstanding. The communal seating and BYOB policy make it a great group spot.
items.tip: It's BYOB with no corkage fee. Bring a bottle of wine and you've got a very affordable dinner at one of the best pizza places in the Southeast.

items.name: Ammazza
items.description: Neapolitan-style pizza in Old Fourth Ward with a 72-hour fermented dough and a massive wood-fired oven. The margherita is a thing of beauty, simple and perfectly charred. The rooftop patio overlooks the neighborhood.
items.tip: No reservations. On weekends, put your name in and walk the BeltLine for 30 minutes while you wait.

items.name: O4W Pizza
items.description: New York-style slices and whole pies in a casual counter-service spot. The slices are big, foldable, and properly greasy in the best way. The pepperoni slice is a go-to for a reason. They also do a mean calzone.
items.tip: Grab two slices and a beer for a quick, satisfying lunch. The white pizza with ricotta is an underrated pick.

items.name: Slim & Husky's
items.description: Creative, thick-crust pizzas with interesting topping combinations in a hip-hop-themed space. The dough is made fresh daily and the specialty pies (like the Cee-Lo Green with pesto) are worth trying. Started in Nashville and the Atlanta location delivers.
items.tip: Build your own if the specialty pies feel too adventurous. The crust is the star here, so keep the toppings simple.

items.name: Junior's Pizza
items.description: A late-night staple near the West End. New York-style slices that are perfect at 1am (and just as good at noon). Nothing fancy, just solid pizza at a fair price. The kind of slice shop every neighborhood needs.
items.tip: Open late. This is your post-bar pizza destination. The cheese slice is all you need.

items.name: Varuni Napoli
items.description: Certified Neapolitan pizza in Midtown using imported Italian ingredients, a custom-built oven, and a dough recipe that produces a beautifully blistered crust. The margherita DOC with buffalo mozzarella is the way to go.
items.tip: The lunch special is an excellent value. Get a personal pizza and a drink for well under $15.

items.name: Nina & Rafi
items.description: A newer spot in Poncey-Highland doing creative neo-Neapolitan pies. The dough is excellent, the toppings are thoughtful, and the natural wine list pairs perfectly. It's a date-night pizza spot done right.
items.tip: The fungi pizza with mixed mushrooms and truffle oil is worth the upcharge. Pair it with something from the natural wine list.

items.name: Edgewood Pizza
items.description: A no-frills slice shop on Edgewood Avenue that's been feeding the late-night crowd for years. The slices are huge, the prices are right, and it's exactly the kind of pizza you want after a night out on Edgewood Avenue.
items.tip: Open very late on weekends. The pepperoni slice at 2am hits different.

items.name: Little Bear
items.description: A Summerhill restaurant that does excellent wood-fired pizzas among other things. The menu is broader than just pizza, but the pies are consistently great. The space is warm and inviting, and the cocktails are strong.
items.tip: Split a pizza and a few small plates for the best experience. The burrata appetizer pairs perfectly with any pie.

items.name: Fred's Pizza
items.description: A Decatur institution doing New York-style pizza since the '80s. It's cash-only, the decor hasn't changed in decades, and the pizza is exactly as good as it's always been. A true original in a sea of newcomers.
items.tip: Cash only. There's an ATM next door. The large cheese pizza is the move for groups.

lists.title: Dog-Friendly Spots in Grant Park
lists.seoTitle: Dog-Friendly Spots in Grant Park Atlanta | atlanta news & talk
lists.seoDescription: The best dog-friendly parks, patios, and spots in the Grant Park neighborhood of Atlanta. Where to take your pup in Grant Park.
lists.intro:
Grant Park is one of the most dog-friendly neighborhoods in Atlanta. Walk around on any given evening and you'll see dogs outnumbering people on some blocks. The neighborhood has great parks, dog-friendly patios, and a community that genuinely loves its four-legged residents.

If you live in Grant Park with a dog (or you're visiting with one), these are the places you need to know about.
lists.imageAlt: Happy dog playing in a park with green grass
lists.imageCredit: Photo by Berkay Gumustekin on Unsplash

items.name: Grant Park Dog Park (Hank Aaron Dog Park)
items.description: The neighborhood's dedicated off-leash dog park, located in the southeast corner of Grant Park near Boulevard. It has separate areas for large and small dogs, water fountains, and shade trees. It's well-maintained and has a strong regular crowd of friendly dog owners.
items.tip: Weekday mornings are the sweet spot. Less crowded than evenings but still enough dogs for yours to socialize with.

items.name: Grant Park Trails
items.description: The park itself has multiple walking paths that loop through the green space. Dogs must be on-leash outside the dog park area, but the trails are wide, shaded, and perfect for a long walk. The full loop is about 1.5 miles.
items.tip: The path near the gazebo and around the perimeter is the quietest route. Avoid the Zoo Atlanta entrance area during peak hours.

items.name: Six Feet Under Pub & Fish House
items.description: A rooftop seafood restaurant with one of the best patios in Grant Park. The rooftop is not dog-friendly, but the ground-level patio welcomes dogs. The fried fish tacos and the views of Oakland Cemetery make it a great stop.
items.tip: Request the ground-floor patio and they'll bring a water bowl for your dog. The fish and chips are the way to go.

items.name: Beacon Atlanta
items.description: A coffee shop and bar on Memorial Drive with a spacious patio that's very dog-friendly. The outdoor seating area is big enough that dogs aren't bumping into other tables. Good coffee in the morning, good beer in the evening.
items.tip: The patio has shade umbrellas and water bowls for dogs. It's a great work-from-patio spot on weekday mornings.

items.name: Dakota Blue
items.description: A neighborhood bar and restaurant with a casual patio that welcomes dogs. The menu is comfort food done well, and the outdoor space is laid-back enough that your dog can relax under the table without stress.
items.tip: The weekend brunch is dog-patio friendly. The chicken and waffles are solid.

items.name: Grant Park Farmers Market
items.description: Held every Sunday at the park, this farmers market is dog-friendly (on leash) and a great place to do a lap with your pup. Local produce, baked goods, coffee vendors, and often a dog treat vendor. It's a neighborhood institution.
items.tip: Get there between 9 and 10am for the best selection. Bring bags and keep your dog close in the crowded aisles.

items.name: Oakland Cemetery
items.description: Atlanta's oldest cemetery is also a beautiful 48-acre greenspace. Dogs are allowed on-leash and the wide paths, old trees, and historic monuments make it one of the most unique walks in the city. It's peaceful and rarely crowded.
items.tip: The cemetery closes at dusk. Respect the grounds and clean up after your dog. The walking paths near the Confederate section have some of the oldest trees.

items.name: Eventide Brewing
items.description: A Grant Park brewery with a large outdoor area and a very dog-friendly policy. Dogs are welcome in the outdoor space, and there's plenty of room for them to hang out while you enjoy a beer. The beer is solid and the vibe is casual.
items.tip: They sometimes host yappy hours and dog-themed events. Check their social media for upcoming ones.
